data.title: D-Wave Quantum: From Zero To 130x Sales - Now What?
data.description:
Investment Thesis

D-Wave Quantum (QBTS) is one of the hottest stocks in the market right now. Not only leading the market, but also leading the quantum sector, and is up more than 80% year-to-date.

In short, this is your go-to quantum stock leader, as investors buy into its quantum computing of annealing (the lowest energy state to solve problems).

From my perspective, beyond an alluring narrative, this is simply not my sort of Inflection setup.

I have no issue with paying up for a high-quality business, but when I'm asked to pay more than 100x sales, it reaches a point where I don't believe it offers me a suitable risk-reward.

Therefore, I remain neutral on QBTS.

Rapid Recap

Back in March, I said,

And even though D-Wave is clearly a leading quantum player, with strong bookings growth, I'm not sold on this opportunity.

For one, I question the sustainability of its recent growth rates. Secondly, having to pay more than 75x rosy-outlook revenues, isn't an opportunity I would feel comfortable backing.

As a result, I remain neutral on this stock.

As you can see above, this stock has been a wild ride.

Consequently, I suspect that many readers and investors will have seen their holdings hit their stop losses since March and have not participated in the gains the stock has recently made.

D-Wave Quantum's Near-Term Prospects

D-Wave builds quantum computers, but not quite like the kind everyone else is working on. Instead of using the ''gate model'' quantum approach, which is more like how classical computers work, they use something called quantum annealing.

The idea is that this method is superior at solving complex optimization problems, such as determining the best route for deliveries or improving factory operations.

This is the bull case, that D-Wave Quantum helps solve problems faster and more efficiently than even the world’s best supercomputers, using way less energy.

The game is how to demonstrate in real-world tasks that they can indeed solve problems. To this, for instance, they are working with Ford (F) to make its car factories more efficient.

Furthermore, their latest Advantage2 system is starting to roll out, while delivering massive leaps in performance, with faster speeds and higher accuracy.

Beyond the car factory floor, they’re getting attention in new areas like blockchain, where their quantum systems could cut electricity use dramatically while boosting machine learning performance.

Essentially, the point is that it’s very early in the story, but there are some tangible signs that point to growing demand.

Given this background, let's now discuss its fundamentals.

Revenue Growth Rates Will Decelerate

D-Wave Quantum doesn't provide investors with guidance.

So, we already knew back in Q4 2024, as the company had provided guidance at the time, that there would be a significant sale made in Q1 2025.

Now that this is in the rearview mirror, investors are thinking, what's next? And that's where the bull case starts to hit a blemish.

As it stands right now, we know that its Advantage2 hardware sales contributed more than 80% of its Q1 2025 revenues.

This means that unless D-Wave makes a massive, similarly scaled sale in the next 2 weeks, this means that this business that reported more than 500% y/y in revenue growth rates in Q1 will see their revenues decelerate to less than 30% y/y.

At which point, we are left with the following: a story stock, but without much growth to support its story. And this surfaces the key question: how much will investors be willing to pay for that?

QBTS Stock Valuation -- 130x Estimated Forward Sales

Earlier this week, D-Wave Quantum announced that it would raise around $400 million of capital via an At-The-Market raise. This would dilute investors by approximately 13%. This raise, together with the roughly $300 million of cash already on the balance sheet, should provide them with ample capital to reach profitability.

Consequently, this could mean that this would be the final round of shareholder dilution in the near term, something that is clearly bullish.

Now, to be clear, nobody has a clue what sort of revenues D-Wave will report in 2025. Not even management. They could sell more Advantage2 annealing quantum computing system, and they could just as well not have any further sales.

But let's say that they do have another hardware sale in 2025, so that their revenues, together with associated professional services, reach an impressive $40 million. That's the best-case scenario.

This is massively higher than analysts' current expectations, and would take analysts by surprise. If that were to happen, the stock is priced at 130x estimated forward sales.

It's not that this is where the story ends, either. Just because you are able to deliver revenues, it does not mean you have a profitable business, right?

As such, I don't see this investment thesis delivering me with a suitable risk-reward.

Risk Factors to the Upside

D-Wave’s approach to quantum computing of annealing, is different from what most of the industry is pursuing (gate-based quantum).

This could see its technology gain rapid traction, and the stock could continue to be a massive winner, even it has relatively little revenue to support its valuation.

Another risk factor to the upside is that over the past decade, we’ve seen stock momentum turn into bubbles. For example, Tilray (TLRY) soared nearly 15x during the 2018 cannabis hype, Plug Power (PLUG) skyrocketed in the 2020 clean energy wave, and GameStop (GME) became a meme-stock rocket in 2021. All surged on just a story. D-Wave Quantum could follow a similar path. With real tech, enterprise traction, and a shot at commercial quantum supremacy, it’s not hard to imagine its stock reaching unprecedented heights if momentum kicks in.

The Bottom Line

While I find D-Wave Quantum’s technology genuinely exciting and its momentum hard to ignore, I’m staying neutral for now.

The company has made impressive strides, and its approach to quantum annealing could unlock serious value down the line.

But with revenue growth likely to slow sharply in the near term—and the stock trading at over 130x even the most optimistic revenue estimates—I just can’t justify the current valuation.

It’s not that I doubt the long-term potential, but I need to see more consistency in sales and a clearer path to profitability before I’m comfortable backing it.

For now, I’ll be watching from the sidelines, ready to re-evaluate if the fundamentals start to catch up with the story.

data.title: Japan Tobacco: Staying Positive On New Product And Non-Core Disposal
data.description:
I'm still bullish on Japan Tobacco Inc. (OTCPK:JAPAY) (OTCPK:JAPAF) (2914.T) or "JTI".

The earlier February 13, 2025, update evaluated JTI's FY24 financials.

My opinion of JAPAY's business portfolio and product line-up changes is favorable. This has prompted me to retain a Buy for the stock.

Eyes on New Brand and Latest Devices

JAPAF's share of the JP ("Japanese") HTP ("Heated Tobacco Product") industry expanded by +1.8 ppts ("percentage points") year-on-year to almost 13% in 1Q25.

Going forward, I think that JTI can seize an even bigger slice of the pie.

A heating unit "Ploom AURA" ("PAURA") and a high-end HTS ("Heated Tobacco Sticks") label "EVO" are the fresh additions to the group's assortment beginning next month.

"Ploom AURA's" Distinguishing Features

As per the chart above, PAURA boasts multiple heating modes that enable smokers to customize the duration and intensity of usage. It also utilizes a "smart heatflow technology" which makes the consumption experience more uniformed throughout the entire process.

JTI's concealed identity study found that over half ("source: May 27, 2025, media briefing") of the research subjects preferred PAURA over other HTPs in terms of "taste". This device is likely to generate robust sales when it's introduced in early-3Q25.

EVO-branded Tobacco Sticks at a Glance

During the same period, JAPAY will start to roll out its premium consumable brand "EVO".

EVO's offerings are meant to be complementary to AURA's "heatflow tech". The new logo's HTS are designed in a way to allow maximum heat exposure for the tobacco leaves.

JAPAF intends to provide ten million EVO samples in the initial round of marketing to obtain customer buy-in. This is to convince people that EVO-branded sticks are "tastier".

JTI's goal to widen its presence in the high-potential segment of the tobacco sector. Its internal research suggests that HTP's worldwide penetration rate ("of the overall cigarette market") will double in the coming decade. JAPAY anticipates that its domestic market share in this category can rise further ("from the current 13") to a"mid-single digit" percentage.

My take is JAPAF's growth ambitions are realistic after analyzing its product portfolio tweaks.

Higher Shareholder Returns and Narrower Conglomerate Discount Are Possible

JAPAY's proposed monetization of its 55 percent interest in "TORII PHARMACEUTICAL CO., LTD." or "TPCL" is scheduled for completion in end-3Q2025.

I see this as a value-accretive move for two major reasons.

Investors are more keen to be vested in pure-plays, since they can do their own diversification at the portfolio-level. JAPAF would naturally be assigned a holdco ("holding company") discount, since it owns non-tobacco operations like pharma and food.

Looking ahead, one of the two non-core businesses will cease to exist for JTI. TPCL, which represented 3% of the group's first quarter topline, is planned to be sold as highlighted above. This leaves JAPAY with just its cigarette and F&B ("4% of 1Q25 revenue") divisions remaining.

The last time JTI did a similar corporate structure simplification move was the sale of its soft drink division 10 years ago.

A more demanding valuation for JAPAF should be justified. This considers the diversion of management attention to the core business and the increased proportion of financial contribution from tobacco.

Separately, TPCL's divestment should put JAPAY in a good position to become more shareholder-friendly with respect to future cash distributions.

I estimate that JTI will receive adjusted ("excluding TORII's existing cash") cash inflows amounting to around JPY 0.05T from this deal.

At its 1Q25 analyst briefing, JAPAF clarified that there's "no change in the policy of the return to shareholders" at "this point of time" despite the TPCL sale.

But JAPAY also mentioned at the latest call that it "would like to enhance the returns to shareholders" in the future with "the improving of our business in the midterm."

In my view, JAPAF's actual dividends for the current year will be a positive surprise.

JTI's existing guidance is for FY2025 DPS ("Dividend Per Share") to be flat at ¥194. I'm predicting a 5% hike instead.

One scenario has JAPAY generating higher-than-expected earnings with the allocation of the TPCL proceeds to "PAURA/EVO" marketing.

The other situation assumes that JAPAF becomes comfortable with a higher DPR ("Dividend Payout Ratio policy at 75% now") with the cashflow infusion from TORII.

You Should Consider These Risks

It's possible JAPAY's new products aren't as popular as what I hoped for. This could be due to stiffer-than-expected competition or a failure to assess customer preferences accurately.

JTI might choose to engage in initiatives that are deemed to be negative by stockholders. Very few would be happy if it acquired non-tobacco assets or did a payout cut.

The Final Word

I remain positive on JAPAF, taking into account its latest cigarette offerings and its pharma stake disposal.

The company's shares are also cheap on a peer comparison.

The mean consensus forward EV/EBITDA metric for Philip Morris (PM) and BAT ("British American Tobacco") (BTI) is 13.6 times. JTI's NTM ("Next Twelve Months") EBITDA multiple is 30% lower at 9.5x ("source: S&P Capital IQ").

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Search Funds: A Strategic Investment In Underserved Markets
data.description:
By Arnold Madanha, CFA

Investors seeking to diversify their holdings away from traditional private equity may want to look at search funds. Although these funds debuted in the mid-1980s, they have gained traction in recent years as the number of funds has grown exponentially and returns have been consistently attractive. This blog looks at search funds — what they are, how they differ from private equity, and why they should be on the radars of some investors.

What are Search Funds?

A search fund is an investment vehicle formed to find, acquire, and operate a closely held business. The fund uses predetermined investment criteria, such as minimum EBITDA and revenue, industry, and geography. The funds were conceived in 1984 by Irv Grousbeck, the MBA Class of 1980 Adjunct Professor of Management at Stanford University’s Graduate School of Business. Since then, over 700 search funds have been launched, creating an entire ecosystem known as entrepreneurship through acquisition (ETA). There are now search funds operating in Europe, Latin America, and Asia.

There are two primary types of search funds: the self-funded and the traditional model. A third, relatively new model, the independent sponsor model, is beginning to gain traction.

In the self-funded model, an entrepreneur uses savings and family contributions to fund expenses such as marketing, subscriptions, and travel. Term loans and government-backed programs usually fund the acquisition, depending on the market in which the entrepreneur operates. However, most self-funded entrepreneurs partner with several investors to finance the equity portion of the deal.

Under the traditional search fund model, the most prevalent, an entrepreneur raises capital by selling units to investors. These units represent an equity stake in the entrepreneur’s search fund. The capital covers search-related expenses for 24 to 36 months. Investors who purchase units at this stage receive the right but not the obligation to participate in financing the acquisition. They will have a right of first refusal to finance the entire equity portion of the acquisition before the entrepreneur approaches outside investors. A board of advisors provides the entrepreneur with guidance and support during the search phase and a full board of directors once the acquisition is made.

The investment horizon post-acquisition ranges from four- to seven-years. Recently, however, search funds have adopted a long-term hold strategy to maximize value creation. The search fund ecosystem is being driven by leading business schools such as the University of Virginia’s Darden School of Business, Harvard, Stanford’s GSB, and the University of Chicago Booth School of Business. These schools identified search funds as a path graduates can take to become CEOs of small businesses.

Search funds target small- to medium-sized businesses (SMBs) in underexplored markets, creating opportunities in areas often overlooked by private equity funds. Unlike private equity, which targets larger businesses with high competition, search funds operate in niches where valuations are lower, and deals are less contested. PE funds also invest in multiple companies, while search funds are designed to invest in a single company. Many search funds tend to target businesses that serve local or regional markets, providing vital goods or services that can be scaled with proper management. Ideal acquisitions are companies that generate consistent positive cashflows, have recurring revenue, low customer churn, minimum EBITDA of $1 million, low exposure to external risks, and a strong management team. The opportunity lies in the value creation ability of the search fund.

The newest type of search fund is the independent sponsor model. This model allows entrepreneurs to pursue acquisitions without raising a traditional search fund upfront. Instead of securing committed capital before searching, independent sponsors identify and negotiate deals first, then raise equity and debt financing from investors on a deal-by-deal basis. This approach offers flexibility, enabling searchers to leverage their networks and expertise while aligning investor interests with specific opportunities.

The Value Proposition

The Stanford Graduate School of Business 2024 Search Fund Study (Figure 1) analyzed the 681 search funds formed in the US and Canada since 1984. The funds reported an internal rate of return (IRR) of 35.1% and a return on investment (ROI) of 4.5x. The consistent performance across decades, despite changing macroeconomic conditions, underscores the resilience and long-term value-creation potential of the search fund model.

Figure 1 | IRR and ROI by Year of Company Acquisition.

Search funds offer a compelling investment model by aligning seamlessly with the long-term, strategic objectives of most investors who prioritize sustainable growth over quick exits. Unlike traditional private equity, search fund entrepreneurs emphasize operational value creation post-acquisition, dedicating themselves to hands-on management and value-add activities that enhance business efficiency and profitability, resulting in stronger operational performance. Search funds target undercapitalized small- to medium-sized businesses, unlocking unique opportunities in underexplored sectors with significant growth potential. This combination of alignment, operational focus, and access to untapped markets positions search funds as an attractive vehicle for investors seeking both financial returns and lasting impact.

Given the role of business schools, there are opportunities for family offices and institutional investors to partner with MBA programs to help cultivate a pipeline of skilled operators while creating search fund accelerators, structured programs offering capital, mentorship, and networks could professionalize the ecosystem and reduce risk.

The Future

The search fund model is gaining momentum, with growing adoption in Europe, Latin America and Asia, alongside rising interest from institutional investors seeking alternatives to traditional private equity. This expansion reflects the model’s appeal: high potential returns from entrepreneurial talent in underserved markets. Technology is poised to accelerate this trend as AI and data-driven tools streamline the funds' search process. Search funds will benefit from faster target identification, due diligence, and enhanced post-acquisition operations through predictive analytics and efficiency gains.

Search funds stand out as a valuable alternative asset class, offering diversification, alpha potential, and operational upside in underserved markets. Their lower capital requirements, hands-on value creation, and alignment with long-term investor goals make them a compelling counterpoint to traditional private equity. In addition to their investment potential, search funds represent an opportunity to back entrepreneurial talent and reshape how value is created in the private markets.

Disclaimer: Please note that the content of this site should not be construed as investment advice, nor do the opinions expressed necessarily reflect the views of CFA Institute.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Frontier Telecom's Hidden Gem: VEON's Undervalued Growth And Nasdaq Catalyst
data.description:
Summary

VEON Ltd. (NASDAQ:VEON) is a leading global digital operator, combining traditional telecommunication services, fast-growing fintech and streaming services across 5 key high growth frontier markets. Its market cap is only $3.3bn, and it is significantly lower than its peers like Bharti Airtel of $130bn and America Movil of $50bn and their subscriber counts are only ~280mil and ~380mil respectively. The market cap is too low in comparison to their total subscribers' count. Thus, I believe there is a fundamental mispricing as the valuation gap is too wide. I rate this a Strong Buy.

Business Model

Essentially, VEON's business model is built on three interlocked pillars: telecommunication services, rapidly expanding digital ecosystem and asset-light structure. The company operates in several emerging economies such as Pakistan, Ukraine, Kazakhstan, Bangladesh, Uzbekistan and Kyrgyzstan. It currently serves ~160mil subscribers.

A majority of its revenue belongs to the telecommunication segment like SMS and data services. Its 4G penetration has climbed to 50-74% users (see below image). This means that there is room for more growth via increasing average revenue per user (ARPU) through premium data plan offerings. VEON offers fixed-line broadband and IPTV. Fixed-line broadband is just a high-speed internet service that is delivered through a fixed wired network, which is mainly through fiber. IPTV is internet protocol television that allows you to watch on demand like Netflix delivers through broadband connections.

Its digital ecosystem is its fast-growing segment. It includes financial services like JazzCash in Pakistan, digital entertainment platforms like Tamasha in Pakistan and AI-driven super apps like MyBL which integrates Telehealth and music streaming. Essentially, VEON is like the MTN Group or Airtel Africa, which, at its core, is a mobile operator in emerging markets while aggressively building fintech and entertainment services.

VEON is also monetizing its infrastructure through tower sale-leasebacks since 2021. For instance, Banglalink sold ~6,000 towers for $100mil in October 2023. This frees up capital and reduces ongoing capex needs. The company has guided capex intensity to decline down to 17-19% for FY25 (see comments from Kaan Terzioglu before Q&A). It helps to free up cash flow, and it is able to reduce its net debt from $8.3bn in 2021 to $2.9bn in 1Q 2025. VEON's AI1440 strategy embeds artificial intelligence into all its services, such as using AI agents to handle routine queries in their mother tongue. The 1440 represents 1,440 minutes in a day of using AI to help serve their customers better through its local language for each geographical market.

Effectively, VEON pulls customers through its strong and affordable connectivity, setting a foundation. Then, it would encourage its community to use its digital apps, either for payments or streaming. By doing that, they are able to drive more ARPU, as a typical consumer would spend 3-4x more than data or voice services. Thus, this creates higher and predictable cash flow and also lifts profitability as the margins are higher in the digital services.

Investment Thesis

VEON sits at the nexus of rapidly expanding telecom frontier markets. The company holds #1 market share for several markets such as Ukraine, Pakistan and Kazakhstan. According to Zero One, the population growth in these key markets and the increasing 4G penetration serve as structural key drivers for higher revenue and margins uplift. In fact, on average, the 4G penetration levels are only from ~50% to ~70%, and the 4G+ penetration % level is even lower. This means that there is a huge market for them to upgrade them to "multiplay" which would uplift ARPU by 3.7x of basic voice subscribers.

This also brings me to another topic where VEON also has its digital service businesses with the potential to reach unicorn status. Its digital business is also doing really well, as its revenue has already increased by 50.2% YoY in Q1'25. This shows that the products are a good fit for its customers and in the long run, it will serve as a strong revenue pillar. In fact, every increase in revenue would also lead to a stronger network effect as they are added into the ecosystem.

Therefore, these factors create a self-reinforcing cycle, as each 4G upgrade for each subscriber would lead to new digital users. These users would drive disproportionate revenue growth through fintech, streaming content and super app adoptions, creating massive value overtime.

VEON's asset-light strategy is also foundational to its value-creation framework. As explained above, in Bangladesh, the company spun off its 6,000 towers portfolio to isolate the heavy capex and operational risk of the asset-intensive business. VEON also forged a strategic infrastructure partnership with Engro Corporation to pool fiber and tower assets, unlocking $563mil in value. This allows VEON to deleverage and also reduce its capex intensity to 17.9% (see 1Q 25 transcript), within its 17-19% guidance band.

Valuation

Currently, VEON only trades at ~3.5x forward EV/EBITDA and ~9.5x forward P/E which is well below its sector median by ~57% and ~50% respectively.

The upcoming Nasdaq listing of Kyivstar via the Cohen Circle de-SPAC not only unlocks VEON's value, but also it creates a standalone market multiple for Ukraine's #1 telco operator. However, this is not yet recognized by the market. Once the F-4 is effective and Cohen Circle shareholders vote, Kyivstar would trade its own higher multiples, given its higher growth and stronger margins.

As investors are only considering seeing the contribution from VEON's consolidated earnings but not recognizing its high-growth segments, the market is giving a conglomerate discount. This is especially so as Pakistani JazzCash's total value transaction has increased 62.8% YoY.

Risk

One of its largest markets, Ukraine, remains exposed to wartime disruption and cybersecurity threats. There was a data breach in Kyivstar last year, which is a profound ESG risk.

Moreover, these frontier markets currencies also introduced FX volatility as the reported currency is in USD and that would be a headwind should the USD strengthen, leading to lower reported results.

Conclusion

VEON offers an attractive asymmetric risk to reward profile setup. It is trading at deep value, and it has a wonderful business with strong runway for both growth and margin uplifts. The company is doing many right things by deleveraging and moving to an asset-light business model, which would boost both ROA and ROIC. Lastly, the pending Kyivstar de-SPAC catalyst would lead to a strong re-rating of the company. I give this a Strong Buy.

data.title: Rates Spark: Geopolitics Add To Bullish Impulses
data.description:
By Padhraic Garvey, CFA & Benjamin Schroeder

The impulse for Treasuries remains tactically bullish

Another strong auction on Thursday as the 30yr went very well. It saw a negative tail of -1.5bp (simplistically 1.5bp through secondary), and the overall auction metrics were good. This is a further bullish impulse for the bond market, as the mini-bull run continues. Certainly, it remains a tactically bullish Treasury market here. The PPI report helped, too. The 0.1% month-on-month outcome shows a clear containment in prices, at least for now. There is no denying that these headlines are bond bullish. We have a bearish view on bonds based on the tax-cutting / fiscal story, but when we see a tactically bullish impulse, we can’t ignore it.

But note for later - if the market is going to react to inflation data like this as it has been, the question is how it will react when we get tariff-impacted inflation ahead and CPI heads for 4% year-on-year. Enjoy the bond bull phase while it lasts, but there are limits...

Bullish impulses from abroad, not enough to take EUR rates below key levels

Even with correlations between US and EUR rates lower than usual over the past weeks, with little data to work off in recent days, EUR rates have been somewhat more susceptible to bullish impulses from abroad again. More so on the long end, starting off with the disappointing GDP figures out of the UK in the morning and later the lower PPI in the US. Overall though, by itself, this is not enough to move rates beyond recent ranges, and the 10y EUR swap rate bounced off the 2.5% level.

European Central Bank officials like Isabel Schnabel reiterated that the central bank's tightening cycle is coming to an end. Now that rates are seen at a neutral level, a pause looks very likely, though some officials do not want to take another cut explicitly off the table. The market will likely remain geared to at least one more cut, given the benign inflation picture. It could be inclined to look at more cuts given remarks from the ECB’s vice president that the central bank is now more concerned about threats to growth than about inflation, but it would probably take clearer signals from the domestic data for that to happen in a way that the short end outperforms the long end.

The notable outperformance of Bunds versus swaps over Thursday’s session already hinted at a flight to safety, as headlines pointed to tensions building around Iran.

Friday’s events and market view

After the final inflation prints for May from Germany, France and Spain in the morning, industrial production data for the bloc covering the month of April is expected to show a month-on-month decline while the trade balance for the same month is also seen dropping back from a March spike.

Probably more relevant for near-term direction is the US data. The University of Michigan consumer sentiment index is seen improving somewhat in June from 52.2 to a consensus of 53.6. That would still leave it at a relatively subdued level, reflecting the still elevated inflation expectations component – the 1y inflation rate is seen coming at 6.4%.

Friday evening has scheduled rating reviews in the eurozone for Germany, Belgium, Finland and Slovakia. The one that stands out is Fitch’s AA- for Belgium, as it carries a negative outlook, but it was last affirmed only this February.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

Original Post

data.title: Full Circle: The Resilience Of Leveraged Finance
data.description:
By Jennifer Schnabl

After Q1 2025 began with positive momentum, Q2 has seen some of the largest bouts of volatility in the recent past. The April 2 tariff announcements sent markets on a series of extraordinary swings. On April 8, the S&P 500® was down 15% YTD and VIX® spiked to 52.33.1 However, positive trade developments that followed the initial announcement, including a trade truce with China on May 12, have resulted in markets retracing the moves from early April, bringing them almost full circle to where they began the year.

Within fixed income, U.S. high yield and bank loans (see Exhibit 1) felt much of the early-April volatility, with high yield spreads widening to 545 bps before retracing to 473 bps by the end of May. Bank loan spreads moved in similar fashion, reaching a YTD high of 462 bps on April 8 before falling back to 350 bps on May 30.2

Despite the volatility of the second quarter, leveraged finance asset classes finished May back in positive return territory on a YTD basis, with high yield at 2.6% and bank loans at 2.1% as of May 30, 2025. This rebound was after both markets held negative returns for over two weeks in mid-April.

Moving into the summer, as the uncertainty surrounding tariff policy remains center stage, it is notable that credit markets have largely erased nearly all their weakness YTD. The number of bank loans trading at par or above stands at 27% as of June 6, a sharp increase from April 30’s level of 3.5%.3 While this is still far from January’s 54% level, the technical support for bank loans remains evident. Additionally, the LSTA reported a record loan trading volume of USD 253 billion in Q1 2025, 2% higher than the previous record in Q1 2020,4 evidencing the continued growth in liquidity and depth of the leveraged loan market. The U.S. leveraged loan market currently stands at USD 1.5 trillion in market value, having grown to rival the size of the U.S. high yield bond market (USD 1.4 trillion),5 a testament to the evolution of the liquidity and tradability of this asset class. With the majority of the market expecting two U.S. Fed rate cuts of 25 bps in 2025,6 leveraged loan indices and high yield bond indices, representing floating rate and fixed rate indices, respectively, may help market participants navigate the path ahead.

1 Source: S&P Dow Jones Indices, Federal Reserve Bank of St. Louis, June 8, 2025.

2 Discount margin to maturity is used for bank loan spread.

3 Source: S&P Dow Jones Indices, as of June 8, 2025.

4 Source: LSTA “1Q25 Loan Trading Volume Report”, April 22, 2025.

5 Source: S&P Dow Jones Indices, U.S. leveraged loan market represented as the USD market value of the S&P UBS Leveraged Loan Index. U.S. high yield bond market represented as the USD market value of the iBoxx USD High Yield Developed Markets Index, as of June 2, 2025.

6 Source: CME Group, June 8, 2025.

Disclosure: Copyright © 2025 S&P Dow Jones Indices LLC, a division of S&P Global. All rights reserved. This material is reproduced with the prior written consent of S&P DJI. For more information on S&P DJI, please visit www.spdji.com. For full terms of use and disclosures, please visit www.spdji.com/terms-of-use.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Veon's Post-Russia Pivot: A Digital Turnaround In Progress
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Veon Ltd. (VEON) is a restructured telco in emerging markets that has shown tangible operational improvements after the Russian exit. The shift to digital services, margin expansion, and leaner cost structure has lifted ROIC and positioned the company for better returns.

However, while Veon has delivered a credible turnaround, its heavy exposure to Ukraine - a single country contributing nearly one-third of adjusted EBITDA - remains a significant geopolitical risk. Moreover, its long-term revenue growth ambitions remain unproven.

Although my valuation indicates a 35% margin of safety, this cushion is too dependent on the contribution of Ukraine. Until Veon can deliver a comparable margin of safety, excluding Ukraine, I would not consider it a buy.

Pivot To Emerging Markets

Veon is a telecommunications and digital services company providing voice, data, and digital services through a range of mobile and fixed-line technologies. In recent years, Veon has also been moving beyond traditional telecom services by investing in digital platforms and adjacent services such as fintech, entertainment, and e-commerce.

Its evolution reflects a broader strategy to diversify revenue streams and capture digital growth in its footprint markets.

Its operational footprint now spans six emerging market countries – Pakistan, Bangladesh, Ukraine, Kazakhstan, Uzbekistan, and Kyrgyzstan. It previously had operations in countries like Russia, Armenia, and Georgia, but has since divested these assets.

As can be seen from Chart 1, Russia was a significant revenue contributor, but the company no longer operated in Russia since 2022. This exit can be attributable to geopolitical risks from the Russia–Ukraine war and sanctions pressures.

This exit also enabled it to reduce its debt significantly as it shifted its resources to the six frontier markets. Since then, Pakistan and Ukraine have ranked No. 1 and 2, respectively, in terms of revenue contribution.

Note to Chart 1: The 2020 revenue was based on the pre-divestment of the Russian operations.

Bullish Aspirations

Management has been bullish about the post-2022 prospects:

“Over the past three years, we have transformed our companies into digital operators and demonstrated that emerging markets are an exciting growth opportunity for global investors. Our aspirations from 2023 through 2027 set the bar higher as we aim for 16%-19% CAGR growth in our Group revenues…” Veon Press Release Jun 2024, in conjunction with its 2024 Capital Markets Day

The company’s high aspirations seem to be based on the following:

  • It transitioned from a traditional telecommunications provider to a digital operator centered on delivering high-margin, scalable services such as mobile financial platforms, video streaming, and other digital lifestyle offerings.
  • Veon has also restructured internally and adopted a decentralized operating model. This localized decision-making is intended to improve responsiveness to market conditions and customer needs.
  • It adopted a capital-light approach to infrastructure. This allows more capital to be redirected into customer acquisition and digital service development, rather than maintaining physical infrastructure.

But I have concerns about whether this double-digit revenue growth rate is overly ambitious, as:

  • Revenue in USD only grew at 3.5% from 2020 (excluding the Russian operations) to 2024.
  • The Q1 2025 USD revenue year-on-year growth rate relative to Q1 2024 was only 8.9%. While the local currency growth was higher at 12.9% (due to forex), as US investors, the focus should be on the USD growth rate.
  • The markets the company operates in seem to have single-digit growth rates.

“The Pakistan Telecom industry, valued at $4.52 billion in 2025, is projected to…CAGR) of 3.28% from 2025 to 2033.” Data Insights Market

“The telecom and pay-TV service revenue in Ukraine was valued at $2.3 billion in 2021 and is forecasted to register a growth of more than 2% during the period of 2022-2026.” Global Data

“The Kazakhstan ICT market, valued at $1.33 billion in 2025…projected to expand at a CAGR of 9.36% from 2025 to 2033…” Data Insights Market

“The Bangladesh telecommunication market, valued at $4.87 billion in 2025, is projected to…CAGR of 4.31% from 2025 to 2033…” Nexa

Better Operating Results Post-Russia

Despite the drop in revenue post-2022, there was gross profit margin expansion. This, in turn, led to better contribution margin post-2022. Refer to Chart 2.

The other positive sign is that the Selling, General and Administration [SGA] margin seemed to hold steady post the Russian exit. This is reflected in the 2023 to 2024 average fixed cost margin of 64% compared to the 2015 to 2019 average of 62%.

Notes to Chart 2:

a. I broke down the operating profits into fixed costs and variable costs.

  • Fixed cost = SGA, R&D and Depreciation & Amortization.
  • Variable cost = Cost of Sales – Depreciation & Amortization.
  • Contribution = Revenue – Variable Cost.
  • Contribution margin = Contribution/Revenue.

b. The metrics in these Charts were based on the financial statements, where for the 2020 and 2021 performances, the Russian operations were treated as assets held for sale i.e. they are not part of the operations.

The better margins resulted in improved operating returns. From 2023 to 2024, the ROIC averaged 16.2% compared to the 2015 to 2019 average of 6.8%.

I believe that these trends are sustainable moving forward:

  • Since exiting Russia, Veon has reshaped its business for more sustainable performance. The company now focuses on six core emerging markets where digital adoption is rising and competition is less intense.
  • Veon is also shifting from a traditional telco to a digital operator, offering higher-margin services. These digital services scale efficiently, driving margin expansion even as overall revenue declines.
  • The company has a leaner structure and increased automation. It has also adopted a capital-light strategy, such as selling towers and sharing networks, to improve returns.

My findings suggest that while there are concerns about double-digit topline growth, there seemed to be a better likelihood for profitability growth.

Improving Peer Performance

I compared Veon's performance with some regional emerging market telcos. Refer to Table 1.

Veon is a small player in terms of the 2024 revenue and ranked No. 4 in terms of the 2020 to 2024 revenue growth rate. Note that the average growth rate for the panel was 2.3% CAGR from 2020 to 2024.

Notes to Table 1:

  • Airtel Africa (AAF). Provides telecommunications and mobile money services in Nigeria, East Africa, and Francophone Africa.
  • Millicom (TIGO). Emerging market telco with mobile, broadband, and fintech offerings operating in Latin America and Africa.
  • MTN Group (MTN). Major pan-African telecom operator with mobile money and digital services in Africa and the Middle East.
  • Turkcell (TCELL). Turkish telco with a strong digital portfolio and Ukraine exposure.
  • Safaricom (SCOM). Best-in-class emerging market telco with mobile money dominance operating in Kenya.

I compared Veon’s 2020 to 2024 performances with those of its peers based on the following metrics. Refer to Charts 3 and 4. Compared to peers, Veon is now credible but still rebuilding trust, especially on earnings quality and cash generation. I would consider Veon as a peer recovery story, but not a leader yet.

  • Veon started in 2020 with among the worst returns on capital. But by 2024, it had improved its return to rank No. 2.
  • Veon also improved its EBIT margin over the review period, ranking No. 2 in 2024 compared to ranking No. 5 in 2020. But this was more due to the peers facing steeper declining trends compared to Veon.
  • Veon has the most volatile free cash flow margin. It had the worst performance in 2021 and 2022 before spiking to be the best in 2023. By 2024, it had the worst performance again.
  • Veon also had the more volatile EPS. For 3 years, it had the worst EPS, while in 2021 and 2024 it had the best EPS.

The above peer comparison and the earlier analysis suggest that there is a good chance for Veon to become a better performer. Structurally and strategically, it is far ahead of where it was in 2020. However, its inclusion among top-tier performers depends on delivering stability in cash flow and earnings over the next 2–3 years.

I would think of Veon today as a restructured contender showing signs of strength, but still needing to prove it can win the long game.

Financially Sound

I would consider Veon financially sound based on the following.

As of the end of March 2025, it has cash of about USD 1.7 billion, equal to about 22% of its total assets.

It has a low Reinvestment rate (Reinvestment/NOPAT) that averaged 23% over the past decade.

  • I defined Reinvestment = CAPEX & Acquisitions – Depreciation & Amortization + increase in Net Working Capital.
  • Over the long run, I would expect the Reinvestment rate to be governed by the fundamental growth equation of growth = Return X Reinvestment rate. Based on the 2022 to 2024 growth rate of 3.6% and the average ROIC of 10%, we would have a fundamental Reinvestment rate of 36%.

Over the past decade, it generated positive cash flow from operations every year with a very good cash flow conversion rate. During this period, it generated a total of USD 22.7 billion in cash flow from operations compared to the total PAT of USD 0.9 billion.

It had a good capital allocation plan, as shown in Table 2. You can see that the cash flow was about enough to fund its CAPEX and acquisitions.

My only concern was its 339% Debt-to-Equity ratio as of the end of March 2025. However, this has come down from its 2020 high of 996% following the exit from the Russian market. Also, its strong cash generating capability as shown in Table 2 mitigates this concern.

Is Veon Fundamentally Sound?

My findings suggest that since its exit from Russia, the company has achieved improved operating returns underpinned by improving margins and good cost control. These trends are supported by the following strategies.

  • Veon has reshaped itself into a leaner, strategically disciplined group concentrated in six high-potential emerging markets.
  • Veon has transitioned from a traditional telco into a digital operator, giving Veon a defensible path to recurring revenue growth and higher customer lifetime value.
  • The company has adopted a decentralized model that empowers local execution and monetizes passive infrastructure like towers to reduce capital intensity.

With a stronger balance sheet, clear focus, and growing digital revenue base, Veon today is far more resilient, efficient, and aligned with long-term value creation than it was before 2022.

Valuation

In my valuation, I have adopted the following picture:

  • The base revenue would be the 2024 revenue growing at 4% in perpetuity.
  • The base contribution margin, fixed cost margin, and capital efficiency would be the 2020 to 2024 average values. Note that these averages excluded the Russian operations.
  • The Reinvestment rate would be based on the fundamental growth equation.
  • The tax rate would be the 2020 to 2024 average tax rate of 44%.

On such a basis, I obtained an intrinsic value of USD 3.26 per share compared to its market price (11 Jun 2025) of USD 2.41 per share. There is a 35% margin of safety.

Valuation model

I valued Veon based on a single-stage Free Cash Flow to the Firm [FCFF] model, as shown in Table 3.

The basic equations used in the model are:

FCFF = EBIT(1 – t) – Reinvestment.

EBIT = Revenue X Contribution margin – Fixed cost

The contribution margin and fixed cost are based on the operating model shown in the right part of Chart 2.

The Reinvestment was based on the fundamental growth equation.

The fixed cost was based on the fixed cost margin.

The WACC used in the valuation model was based on the first page results of a Google search for “Veon WACC”. Refer to Table 4.

Valuation Risks

I think that my assumptions are conservative, comparing the historical trends vs those projected by the valuation model.

  • The projected free cash flow starts at a lower value than that projected by the log normal trend line (shown in dotted line in the right part of Chart 5).
  • While I have projected a steeper revenue trend line compared to the historical trend, the projected contribution margin appears reasonable. Refer to the left part of Chart 5.

I also found the following when comparing the values projected by the valuation model with the historical ones:

  • 9% return on capital compared to the 2020 to 2024 average ROIC of 10%.
  • 25% EBIT margin compared to the 2020 to 2024 average EBIT margin of 25%.

You may think that with a 35% margin of safety based on conservative assumptions, it would be a good buy.

My main concern is that in 2024, Ukraine accounted for 23% of its revenue and 31% of its adjusted EBITDA. We are all aware of the ongoing Ukraine war, and the company has noted the risk.

“…the Company… will continue to be negatively impacted by the consequences of the Russian government’s invasion of Ukraine…that may cast significant doubt…on the Company’s ability to continue as a going concern.” 2024 Form 20F.

It is challenging to build this risk into the valuation model. I think one simpler way is to look for a higher margin of safety. Accordingly, I would be looking for a 30% margin of safety, assuming zero contribution from the Ukraine operations. In this context, assuming that the 2024 revenue is reduced by 23%, all else being the same, the intrinsic value drops to USD 2.64 per share.

Having said that, is there any upside? My margin of safety is based on a revenue growth rate that is significantly lower than management’s aspirational growth rate.

It is obvious that if I valued Veon based on a double-digit growth rate, the margin of safety will be much higher. As a conservative value investor, I would wait for signs of this growth rate before adopting such an aggressive growth rate in my valuation.

Conclusion

Veon’s strategic pivot away from Russia and towards high-growth frontier markets represents a bold transformation from a legacy telecom player to a digital-first operator.

The company has made meaningful progress since then.

  • Despite the drop in revenue post-2022, there was gross profit margin expansion.
  • From 2023 to 2024, the ROIC averaged 16.2% compared to the 2015 to 2019 average of 6.8%.

However, its ambitious revenue growth targets appear optimistic given the modest growth rates of its core markets and the continued volatility in foreign exchange.

Overall, Veon appears fundamentally stronger post-2022, but sustained success will hinge on its ability to convert digital initiatives into consistent USD revenue growth across its emerging market footprint.

While my valuation provided a 35% margin of safety, because about 1/3 of its 2024 EBITDA came from Ukraine, I do not think that this is a good enough margin of safety.

data.title: NCZ: Convertibles Should Be Superior To Traditional Bonds
data.description:
The Virtus Convertible & Income Fund II (NYSE:NCZ) is a closed-end fund that provides investors with a way to obtain a high level of current income without the need to invest in fixed-income securities. This is something that has proven to be quite valuable over the past few years as a combination of excessively low interest rates and inflation have caused many fixed-income securities to fail in their basic task of preserving an investor’s wealth while providing a relatively low return in exchange for the investor putting off spending their money on consumption until a future date. We can see this quite clearly by looking at the inflation-adjusted total returns provided by a few different fixed-income indices over the past ten years:

This chart shows the total returns of the Vanguard Total Bond Market Index Fund (VBMFX), SPDR Bloomberg High Yield Bond ETF (JNK), and iShares Preferred and Income Securities ETF (PFF) over the past ten years, assuming that an investor had a 0% tax rate (i.e. used a retirement or tax-advantaged account to hold the index funds) and reinvested all dividends, distributions, and coupon payments made by the constituent securities into additional shares of the index. In addition, I adjusted the numbers downward to maintain constant dollars using the Consumer Price Index for All Urban Consumers. Thus, if that government-produced index accurately measures the rate of inflation, then the numbers shown above reflect the total real returns that investors received over the past ten years. As we can clearly see, the figures are terrible for all three fixed-income assets:

Fixed-Income Security Type

Average Annual Real Return

Total Real Return Over The Period

Investment-Grade Bonds

-1.46%

-13.71%

Junk Bonds

0.88%

9.14%

Preferred Stocks

0.06%

0.60%

The best performance out of the group came from junk bonds, and even they were not capable of producing an average real return of over 1% per year. Preferred stocks barely managed to keep up with inflation, while investment-grade bonds actually delivered a negative real return. An investor who actually held these securities in a standard taxable account ended up losing purchasing power with all three asset classes.

It is worth noting, too, that the Consumer Price Index for all Urban Consumers may not accurately measure inflation. As Adam Sharp, financial writer and Fed watcher, points out at the Daily Reckoning, the Consumer Price Index for all Urban Consumers states that automobile prices are up 100% since 1980. The median price for an automobile in 1980 was $7,200. In 2023, the median price for an automobile was $48,000. That is a 566% increase, not a 100% increase as the Consumer Price Index claims. Thus, a solid case can be made for a claim that the Consumer Price Index itself understates the actual amount of inflation within the economy. If this is correct, then all of the calculated real returns shown above for investment-grade bonds, junk bonds, and preferred stocks are higher than reality. In other words, if the Consumer Price Index for All Urban Consumers understates inflation, then all of the fixed-income indices shown above delivered negative real returns over the past ten years.

Retirees, in particular, cannot afford to have negative real returns in their portfolios. As their money has to last for the remainder of their lives, it is necessary to at least keep up with inflation because otherwise, they will eventually run out of money. Thus, fixed-income assets would have been a very poor solution for them over the past decade. It is certainly possible that this will continue to be the case going forward, as most projections indicate that the fiscal position of most developed countries will deteriorate going forward, which will likely force the central banks of these nations to institute a form of yield curve control. In that environment, the central bank prints money in order to purchase long-term bonds and force yields down, but it also ends up destroying the value of the currency in the process. Thus, bonds, preferred stocks, and anything else that simply provides a fixed payment of fiat currency on a periodic basis to its owner will deliver negative real returns as investors get paid back with increasingly worthless currency.

The Virtus Convertible & Income Fund II invests in assets that should do a better job at resisting the loss in purchasing power that accompanies inflation. This is because many of the securities in the portfolio are convertible bonds that are linked to equities. As we can see here, convertible securities accounted for about 58.53% of the fund’s portfolio at the end of March 2025:

Equities represent ownership positions of profit-making enterprises. They are not simply a contractual arrangement to pay a specific amount of fiat currency to an investor over a period of time. In theory, a business should be able to grow its revenues and profits at the rate of inflation or greater, thus allowing the common equity of that company to maintain its purchasing power over the long term. This is also true with convertible bonds, as the conversion feature becomes more valuable as the common stock of the issuing company rises. Thus, the convertible bond itself becomes more valuable. We can actually see this by comparing the performance of the iShares Convertible Bond ETF (ICVT), which tracks an index of domestic convertible bonds, with the S&P 500 Index (SP500):

If we add the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index (AGG) to the chart, we get this:

As we can see, the domestic convertible bond index tracks equities much more closely than it does bonds. As such, we can expect convertible bonds to, on average, do a much better job of maintaining an investor’s purchasing power over time than we can expect from most other fixed-income securities. This should give the Virtus Convertible & Income Fund II an advantage over a standard fixed-income fund over the long term.

Recent Performance Update

We last discussed the Virtus Convertible & Income Fund II on October 23, 2024. As such, about seven months have passed since that time, and, as we all know, a lot can happen in seven months. In this case, there have certainly been a number of things that occurred during the period. A few of these events would have had an impact on the performance of the convertible securities and junk bonds that make up the bulk of this fund’s holdings, while other things would have had an impact on equities (which indirectly influence the performance of convertible bonds).

One of the most notable events that occurred during the intervening seven-month period between our last discussion on this fund and today is that the United States concluded its national elections, which resulted in Republican Donald Trump taking the presidency and the Republican Party establishing control of both houses of Congress. This was generally good for the markets, due simply to the fact that investors expected that the Republican winners would roll back some regulations that were imposed on business by the previous administration. However, the prediction markets had been projecting this outcome since late September (Trump was predicted to win on Polymarket in mid-September) so the markets already priced some of the expected policy changes into stocks by the time that my previous article on this fund was published in late October. Overall, the S&P 500 Index rose by 1.45% between October 23, 2024, and the end of the year, with most of the gains coming after the election:

It has been something of a different story this year, however. In mid-January, Hangzhou DeepSeek Artificial Intelligence Co. (DEEPSEEK) released a chatbot powered by generative artificial intelligence that was far less resource-intensive than most American products. This caused the market to doubt its previous predictions of artificial intelligence deployments driving massive hardware sales. Companies such as NVIDIA (NVDA), which had previously been one of the companies responsible for a significant portion of the market strength over the 2023 to 2025 period, sold off, with the company’s shares falling 19.29% during the first three months of 2025:

While the S&P 500 Index was also down over that period, it was mostly a technology selloff. The ProShares S&P 500 ex-Technology ETF (SPXT) only declined 0.51% over the same period. Thus, much of the weakness was found in the technology sector. That was not necessarily a great thing for the Virtus Convertible & Income Fund II, of course, due to the simple fact that the technology sector accounts for 17.48% of the fund’s assets and is its largest single sector holding:

This is a lower allocation to the technology sector than the S&P 500 Index itself possesses, but it is still large enough that a sell-off in technology would cause the price of this fund’s assets to decline. The Virtus Convertible & Income Fund II mostly gets its exposure to the sector through convertible bonds though, as opposed to through equities, so even if the stock price of one of the companies that issued a bond that it owns falls enough to render the conversion feature worthless, the future stream of cash flow provided by the bond still has some value. This is one of the advantages that convertible bonds have over equities. A convertible bond is, at its core, still a bond, so it does not usually decline as much as the linked equity will in a market sell-off.

Finally, one other major event that happened over the past seven months was the market crash that followed the announcement that the United States was imposing tariffs on much of the rest of the world. This event temporarily devastated the common equity markets, as well as the bond markets. One of the things that made this market sell-off interesting is that U.S. Treasury securities did not benefit from the “flight to safety” trade that would ordinarily occur when investors are fearful. This strengthens the case that bonds in general could be a dangerous asset to hold. I have discussed this in a few recent articles (see here, here, and here), so there is no reason to discuss it further right now. The Virtus Convertible and Income Fund II should have some insulation from the bond market’s challenges anyway because many of the securities in its portfolio are directly linked to equities.

Overall, the share price of the Virtus Convertible & Income Fund II is down 0.32% since our last discussion:

As we can see, the fund’s performance compares somewhat poorly to that of both the S&P 500 and the convertible bond index. This is overall quite disappointing, as we would have expected, it to have delivered a positive return, given that both indices were actually up over the period. The fact that it did not may turn off some investors, since nobody likes to underperform the benchmark indices. It is, however, not unusual for this fund to underperform, given that its share price performance during most extended periods has left something to be desired. This is summarized in this chart:

Trailing Period

NCZ Share Price Performance

One-Year

+6.60%

Three-Year

-3.75%

Five-Year

-19.63%

Ten-Year

-59.90%

During all periods, the fund’s share price underperformed both the S&P 500 Index and the domestic convertible bond index. Thus, investors in this fund are quite used to poor share price performance.

The fund tends to do a lot better when we look at the total return. This chart shows the fund’s total return since our last discussion:

The Virtus Convertible & Income Fund II has delivered a total return of 7.84% since our last discussion on October 23, 2024. This was, in fact, better than what the S&P 500 Index (SP500TR) delivered, as well as being better than the total return of the domestic convertible bond index. Thus, just because the share price performance has not been fantastic, it does not mean that investors are not benefiting here.

Peer Distribution Comparison

One of the reasons for the strong total return performance is that the Virtus Convertible & Income Fund II boasts a remarkably high distribution yield. As of the time of writing, this fund boasts an impressive 11.61% yield, which is significantly higher than any of the major domestic fixed-income indices:

Index/ETF

Current Yield

Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index

3.84%

Bloomberg High Yield Very Liquid Index

6.62%

Bloomberg U.S. Convertible Cash Pay Bond > $250 MM Index

2.17%

This gives the Virtus Convertible & Income Fund II an advantage over these indices when it comes to total return. Total return, after all, is the combination of both capital appreciation and any dividends, distributions, or other payments made by the asset to its investors. The fact that this fund has a higher yield than the indices means that it will receive a greater boost from its distribution. Of course, if the share price constantly underperforms the indices, as has been the case for this fund for quite some time, then this advantage will be somewhat muted.

The yield of the Virtus Convertible & Income Fund II also compares reasonably well with that of other closed-end convertible bond funds in the market. We can see this in this peer comparison chart:

Fund Name

Morningstar Classification

Current Yield

Virtus Convertible & Income Fund II

Fixed Income – Taxable-Convertibles

11.61%

Advent Convertible and Income Fund (AVK)

Fixed Income – Taxable-Convertibles

11.79%

Bancroft Fund (BCV)

Fixed Income – Taxable-Convertibles

6.86%

Calamos Convertible Opportunities and Income Fund (CHI)

Fixed Income – Taxable-Convertibles

11.00%

Ellsworth Growth and Income Fund (ECF)

Fixed Income – Taxable-Convertibles

6.32%

As we can clearly see, the Virtus Convertible & Income Fund II is one of the two funds in this peer comparison with the highest yields. Technically, the Advent Convertible and Income Fund has a higher yield as of the time of writing, but the two funds are only eighteen basis points apart in terms of yield. This is a small enough difference that it is possible for fluctuations in either direction to reverse the relative order of these two funds. Historically, though, the two funds do tend to track each other rather closely:

We can see that over the past three years, the Virtus Convertible & Income Fund II and the Advent Convertible and Income Fund have had pretty similar share price performance. As such, we can probably expect that the Advent Convertible and Income Fund will usually be slightly higher in terms of yield.

This does not, however, necessarily mean that the Virtus Convertible & Income Fund II can actually sustain its current distribution. The market is not pricing in a distribution cut, but as we can see here, the fund’s net asset value is down 2.71% year-to-date:

Thus, the fund has failed to cover its distribution since the start of the year. This is troubling for anyone who is seeking to generate a safe and secure income from their assets, but it is in line with what the fund has historically done. This fund has steadily decreased its distribution since its inception in 2003:

Thus, the fund has historically over-distributed, which has resulted in a steady deterioration of its net asset value and its distribution. This could be problematic if the goal is to simply purchase shares and then receive a spendable income from the fund. However, it may not be too bad if an investor can reinvest some of the distributions that they receive in order to acquire more shares over time to offset the per-share distribution and net asset value deterioration.

About The Fund

The fund’s website states that the Virtus Convertible & Income Fund II has the primary objective of providing its investors with a very high level of total return. This makes sense given the fund’s strategy of:

Investing in a diversified portfolio of domestic convertible securities and high-yield bonds rated below investment grade.

The fund invests in convertible bonds and junk bonds, which is not unusual. We have seen this in a number of other funds, including many of the Calamos funds. Overall, it is generally a good strategy, as convertible bonds tend to be less volatile than common stocks but still have equity upside, while junk bonds usually have higher yields than other fixed-income securities and so can provide the fund with income. If executed properly, this can provide superior total returns as most bond funds along with lower volatility than most equity funds.

Unfortunately, take a look at the total real returns that were delivered by this fund over the past decade:

As shown, assuming that all distributions were reinvested into additional shares of the fund and after adjusting for inflation, the Virtus Convertible and Income Fund II only delivered a total real return of 1.08% over the past decade. On average, this fund only managed to beat inflation by 0.11% annually. That is better than investment-grade bonds or preferred stocks managed, but it is inferior to the total real return provided by the junk bond index. Thus, it does not appear that this fund managed to deliver on its potential. As I noted in my previous article, however, it does appear that it has been improving since early 2024. The above chart reflects this too, as we can see its performance starting to improve around that time.

Here is the same chart, this time with the starting date changed to December 31, 2023:

Once again, after adjusting for inflation, we see that the Virtus Convertible & Income Fund II delivered a 16.48% total real return. That is much better than any of the fixed-income indices over the same period. It also did pretty well at protecting its investors against the loss of purchasing power that has occurred over the past eighteen months.

The largest positions in the fund include several technology companies, but we also see utility companies, an entertainment company, and a bank among the fund’s largest positions:

This list includes quite a few changes from the last time that we discussed this fund. In particular, Affirm Holdings (AFRM), Zillow Group (Z), Coinbase (COIN), and Parsons Corporation (PSN) have all been removed from their former positions among the fund’s largest positions. In their place, we have Boeing (BA), Live Nation Entertainment (LYV), Lumentum Holdings (LITE), and MicroStrategy (MSTR). Lumentum produces optical products for cloud computing and industrial applications, so it could be considered a technology company in a sense, but it is clearly not in the same industry as Amazon (AMZN), Microsoft (MSFT), or Apple (AAPL). MicroStrategy has been in the news a lot recently due to its recent shift towards being a Bitcoin Treasury company. Bitcoin (BTC-USD) investors like it because it is now one of the only publicly traded companies that holds Bitcoin as its primary treasury reserve asset, which has certainly benefited the company lately as Bitcoin has risen 13.91% year-to-date:

MicroStrategy has done even better, delivering a 31.12% gain since the start of the year:

Buying MicroStrategy’s convertible bonds may have been a good trade on the part of this fund, given this strong year-to-date performance. We should keep in mind, though, that Bitcoin has been rather volatile historically, so there is a very real chance that the company’s stock will give up some of these gains if some event causes the price of Bitcoin to collapse. I will admit, though, that I am unsure what such a catalyst may be, and there may not even be one in the near future.

Distribution Analysis

The primary objective of the Virtus Convertible & Income Fund II is to provide its investors with a high level of total return. As is the case with most closed-end funds, this one primarily aims to deliver total returns to its investors through the payment of regular distributions. As of the time of writing, these distributions are $0.12 per share monthly ($1.44 per share annually). This gives the fund an 11.61% yield at the current share price.

Unfortunately, as we saw earlier, there could be some reasons to believe that the Virtus Convertible & Income Fund II will not be able to sustain its distributions going forward. The fund’s net asset value is down year-to-date, and this fund has a history of cutting its distribution on a regular basis. Let us have a look at its finances and see how well it is actually doing.

As of the time of writing, the most recent financial document available for the Virtus Convertible & Income Fund II is the annual report for the full-year period that ended on January 31, 2025. This is a newer report than what was available at the time of our last discussion, so let us have a look at it for an update.

For the full-year period that ended on January 31, 2025, the Virtus Convertible & Income Fund II reported a net investment income of $11.710 million. This was not sufficient to cover the $27.402 million that the fund paid out in distributions over the period. This may be somewhat discouraging, but it is not unexpected. After all, convertible bonds deliver their total returns through both direct payments to their owners and through capital gains. Capital gains are not included in net investment income, so any fund that has significant capital gains will likely not be able to cover its distributions solely with net investment income.

For the twelve-month period that ended on January 31, 2025, the Virtus Convertible & Income Fund II reported net realized gains of $25.410 million. When we combine these capital gains with the fund’s reported net investment income, we arrive at a total of $37.120 million. That was more than enough to fully cover the distribution and still leave the fund with quite a lot of money left over. Overall, it did manage to cover its distributions over the period. Thus, at least for now, everything seems to be okay, but we should not ignore the fact that the fund’s net asset value is still down year-to-date.

Valuation

Shares of the Virtus Convertible & Income Fund II are currently trading at a 9.54% discount on net asset value. This is a much better price than the 7.83% discount that the fund’s shares have had on average over the past month.

The Virtus Convertible & Income Fund II historically trades at a discount, which is likely due to its rather disappointing long-term performance history. Over the past three years, the fund has traded at prices ranging from a 6.19% discount to a 17.48% discount. The average price over the three-year period was an 11.36% discount, which is a better price than the fund has today.

Here is how the current price of the Virtus Convertible & Income Fund II compares to its peers:

Fund Name

Premium/(Discount)

Virtus Convertible & Income Fund II

(9.54%)

Advent Convertible and Income Fund

(2.74%)

Bancroft Fund

(12.30%)

Calamos Convertible Opportunities and Income Fund

3.03%

Ellsworth Growth and Income Fund

(12.73%)

As we can see, four of the five funds are trading at fairly large discounts on net asset value. Of the funds that are trading at a discount, the Virtus Convertible & Income Fund II is actually one of the more expensive ones, although it is the median fund overall. Thus, the price does not appear to be too bad relative to the fund’s peers.

Historically, this fund has had a better price than it has today, so it might be possible to wait for a little while and get in at a better price. Overall, though, this one is not too bad right now.

Expense Ratio

The fund’s annual report states that the Virtus Convertible & Income Fund II has an expense ratio of 2.55%:

The annual report also states that the fund had an expense ratio of 1.33% if the interest expenses are excluded.

Conclusion

In conclusion, there are cracks forming in the traditional bond markets, which appear to be due to investors increasingly doubting the ability of governments to afford their debt obligations over the long term without resorting to money printing or defaults. This currency destruction will almost certainly result in investors in longer-term bonds receiving a negative real return on their investments. Convertible bonds should be able to avoid this unfortunate scenario, though, due to their link to equities that should be able to maintain long-term purchasing power. The Virtus Convertible & Income Fund is one way to gain exposure to convertible bonds along with a high yield. Admittedly, though, this fund does have something of a poor performance history, but it seems to have improved over the past eighteen months or so.

data.title: FGDL: Add This Responsible Gold ETF To Your Watchlist
data.description:
The Franklin Responsibly Sourced Gold ETF (NYSEARCA:FGDL) is one of my top picks for investors looking to enter the gold market. The fund seeks to provide investors exposure to the gold spot price while adhering to the latest environmental and social governance (ESG) sourcing standards. It holds physical gold bullion in a JPMorgan Chase Bank N.A, which is an LMBA-accredited vault located in London, U.K.

My view is that FGDL will become one of the most popular ETFs for gold investors due to its already highly competitive expense ratio at 0.15% (which is likely to shrink as the fund's AUM rises), as well as mitigating policy risk from the U.S. SEC's conflict-minerals rules and the EU's Corporate Sustainability Due-Diligence Directive (CSDDD). Being ESG compliant also provides potential liquidity benefits through higher inflows, as it is the only 'certified' gold ETF on the market.

I rate FGDL as a Hold given that there are still some more competitively priced gold ETFs on offer, but I am tempted to upgrade it to a Buy swiftly if regulations tighten around sourcing requirements.

Overview

FGDL was launched in 2022 and is presently the only U.S. physical gold ETF that meets the LBMA Responsible Gold Guidance. The fund tracks the LMBA Gold Price PM, which is accepted as a defacto standard for gold spot pricing.

There are a few points of my argument that the gold sourcing standards of the LMBA Responsible Gold Guidance will become mainstream for every investor looking to invest in gold investment products.

First, it should be noted that many gold ETFs already follow certain ESG-related policies such as the LBMA Good Delivery List, which accredits bars of gold and silver for trade on the global OTC market. This includes instruments such as the SPDR Gold Shares ETF (GLD) and the iShares Gold Trust ETF (IAU). This implies then that these ETFs are already adjacent to more comprehensive ESG policies such as Version 9 of the Gold Guidance policy and that such a shift towards long-term ethical sourcing and robust tracking is becoming the norm, rather than an optional risk management step.

The second reason is that the cost of fully complying with the LMBA Responsible Gold Guidance is relatively minimal when it is passed onto investors, as evidenced by FGDL's expense ratio. Among the most-capitalized gold ETFs by market cap, FGDL has the second-lowest expense ratio at 0.15%, while the most-capitalized ETF, GLD, has an expense ratio of 0.40%, and is not fully ESG compliant.

The main trade off that investors face with FGDL is that its assets under management is just $233.3 million, a fraction of many others.

ETF Ticker Fund Name Expense Ratio AUM (USD)
FGDL Franklin Responsibly Sourced Gold ETF 0.15% $233.3 million
GLD SPDR Gold Shares 0.40% $101.48 billion
IAU iShares Gold Trust 0.25% $46.95 billion
SGOL abrdn Physical Gold Shares ETF 0.17% $5.158 billion
PHYS Sprott Physical Gold Trust 0.41% $11.95 billion
GLDM SPDR Gold MiniShares Trust 0.10% $15.39 billion

What this means in practice is that if the gold market is showing signs of stress and panic, investors may find it harder to fill their orders at their desired prices. FGDL is also not optionable, which may deter income investors from turning their shares into streams of income through selling options.

Comparative Analysis

I believe FGDL shows the market that there is a considerable amount of glut in the expense ratios of gold ETFs, as it is less than half of GLD and PHYS, and these ETFs also offer investors less visibility and peace of mind when it comes to custody risk. A large benefit of FGDL's approach is that every bar is accounted for and each is ethically sourced. Even if investors are unmoved by what occurs in the mines, I think that most will appreciate the added certainty knowing that FGDL has taken more robust risk management steps than its peers.

In terms of performance, there are some very minor differences in performance over the past five and one year, as each ETF tracks the spot price of gold.

However, over much longer time periods, say 10+ years or longer, the 0.25% difference in expense ratio can add up to a significant amount if one was invested in GLD. Just by changing to a more cost-efficient ETF, one could potentially save hundreds of dollars whilst still tracking the same underlying asset. Assuming no growth, a $10,000 investment in GLD would cost around $250 more in fees in today's dollars than FGDL. If one assumes a 7% return that figure rises to around $370.

Gold Outlook

Despite the considerable growth in the gold price over the past twelve months, my view is that the backdrop for continued spot increases is more than likely on the cards.

A key reason I believe that gold will continue to surge in price is that institutional buyers are continuing to buy up tonnes of gold this year. Specifically, around 1,000 tonnes are pegged for purchase. This in turn has led some analysts to provide a forecast of $3,100 to $3,900 per ounce by the end of the year. I concur with analysts' expectations that a substantial increase is on the cards.

My view is that the central banks of primary gold buyers such as China, India, Turkey, and others will continue the "de-dollarization" trend as geopolitical tensions rise. I believe that central banks will continue to purchase gold as part of their long-term strategy to undermine the U.S. currency.

Risks

The main risk for investors investing in FGDL comes from this relatively small funds under management compared with other choices on the market. There is a minor but substantial risk that Franklin Templeton could wind up the ETF if it does not attract sufficient inflows.

Another risk is that gold is showing an extreme overbought rating at 85. Volume remains strong, but the RSI is beginning to flatten on the monthly charts. This suggests that there's a heightened risk of a stall or pullback in the short to medium-term.

Conclusion

FGDL offers a compelling value proposition for investors, especially for those looking to invest in ethically sourced gold. The value investors receive from the fund could far outweigh the minor difference in expense ratio of the cheapest instruments, and it's probable that as its AUM increases, its expense ratio could shrink as well, as especially as the gold market becomes more competitive.
